result:
# The Peculiar Comfort of Unfinished Things

I have been thinking lately about the drawer in my kitchen — or rather, the drawer that exists in almost every kitchen I have ever known. It is not a specific drawer belonging to a specific person. It is more of a universal drawer, a category of drawer, the one that holds the batteries of unknown charge and the takeout menus for restaurants that closed years ago and the single key that no one can remember the purpose of but that everyone is too afraid to throw away. What if it matters? What if the day you discard it is the day you discover what it opens?

There is something I find genuinely comforting about that drawer. Not in spite of its chaos, but because of it.

We spend enormous energy completing things. Finishing projects, closing loops, achieving resolution. Self-help culture is essentially a long argument against the junk drawer — organize your life, eliminate the unnecessary, keep only what sparks joy. And I understand the appeal. A clean surface does something pleasant to the mind. Order creates a kind of breathing room.

But I wonder if we have overcorrected. I wonder if the relentless drive toward completion has made us strangers to one of the quieter human pleasures: the unfinished thing sitting patiently in the corner of your life, asking nothing of you.

Consider the book you started two years ago and left face-down on the nightstand. The conventional guilt around this is enormous. You *should* finish it. You *should* either commit or abandon it properly. But there is another way to look at it. That book is a door that remains open. Somewhere inside it, characters are suspended in mid-conversation, a plot is coiled and waiting, a sentence you haven't read yet might be the most beautiful sentence you will ever encounter. The unfinished book is pure potential. The finished book is wonderful, yes, but it is also over. It has collapsed from possibility into a fixed, specific thing.

This is not an argument for laziness. I want to be careful about that. There are things that genuinely must be finished — promises made to other people, obligations that someone else is depending on. Leaving those things incomplete is not romantic, it is just irresponsible. But the private, personal unfinished things — the half-learned language, the melody you figured out on a piano and never wrote down, the essay you drafted and never published — these seem to me worth reconsidering.

I taught myself the beginning of a song on guitar when I was about nineteen. Just the opening, maybe thirty seconds of music. I never learned the rest. For fifteen years I have been picking up guitars at parties or in music shops and playing those same thirty seconds, and every single time something good happens in my chest. It is not pride exactly. It is more like greeting an old friend. That fragment of music exists in my life in a way that the complete song probably never would. It became mine precisely because I never finished learning it, never moved on.

There is a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi* — the beauty found in imperfection and impermanence. A cracked tea bowl repaired with gold, a garden where moss has taken over the stone. The philosophy suggests that wear and incompleteness carry their own kind of beauty, that the attempt to achieve perfect wholeness misunderstands what makes things precious. I am not sure I have the philosophy right in all its details, but the feeling behind it resonates. The crack is not the failure of the bowl. The crack is the bowl's autobiography.

What I am circling around, I think, is the question of what we lose when we treat our lives as problems to be solved rather than textures to be inhabited.

A solved life would be efficient. Every project completed, every question answered, every relationship brought to a satisfying and articulate conclusion. It sounds appealing in the abstract. In practice I think it would feel like a museum — impressive, and very quiet, and not quite alive.

The unfinished things keep us in conversation with our past selves. That junk drawer is a relationship with every previous version of you who put something in it and thought, *I'll deal with this later.* The unread book is a handshake with the person you were when you bought it, full of intention. The half-learned song is a thread running back to who you were at nineteen, still connected, still humming.

I have a folder on my computer called, with very little creativity, "Old Stuff." It contains documents that span roughly twenty years. Fragments of stories, abandoned essays, jokes that didn't go anywhere, observations I found interesting and then forgot about entirely. I almost never open this folder. But I never delete it either. Its existence does something for me that I find difficult to name. It is proof, maybe. Proof that I have been trying, imperfectly and inconsistently, for a long time. Proof that the person sitting here right now is made of accumulated attempts, not all of which came to anything.

Maybe that is the real function of the unfinished thing. It is evidence of a life in process. A life that is not yet concluded, not yet organized into its final form, not yet ready to be fully interpreted.

We will all be finished eventually. The loop will close. The drawer will, one way or another, be cleaned out.

Until then, I think I will keep the key whose purpose I have forgotten. I will leave the book face-down on the nightstand. I will play my thirty seconds of guitar and feel that small, reliable warmth in my chest.

The finished things tell us who we were.

The unfinished things remind us that we are still here, still in the middle of something, still — improbably, wonderfully — going.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
